content.full_text:
September 28, 1994




Honorable Toni I. Gallagher
Councilmember
City of Dana Point
34365 Dana Strand Road #3
Dana Point, CA 92629

Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-94-279

Dear Councilmember Gallagher:

     This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard-
ing your responsibilities as a member of the Dana Point City
Council under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").1

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTIONS

A. Headlands Specific Plan

     1.     Assuming that you have a conflict of interest under the
Act with regard to land use decisions that affect the Headlands

_____________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000-18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 507 ● Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 2


Specific Plan Area, to what extent may you participate in such
decisions before the city council?

2. To what extent may you participate in informal
discussions about the Headlands Specific Plan Area, if a member of
the public addresses that topic at a council meeting?

3. If you have a conflict of interest by virtue of your
property, may you seek advice or information from the city
attorney regarding the Headlands Specific Plan?

4. Does the Act restrict your spouse's involvement in the
campaign regarding the referendum on the November ballot
concerning the Headlands Specific Plan?

5. Does the Act restrict your involvement in the campaign
regarding a referendum on the November ballot concerning the
Headlands Specific Plan, including:

a. Communicating with the press (including letters to
the editor) and public.

b. Speaking at referendum meetings.

c. Fundraising for the referendum; signing ballot
arguments; involvement with the "No" committee.

B. Solag Disposal Waste Co.

1. Should income owed by Solag Disposal Waste Co. to your
business be disclosed on your assuming office statement?

2. If the city's contract with Solag Disposal Waste Co. is
considered, may you participate in the decision?

CONCLUSIONS

A. Headlands Specific Plan

1 and 2. You are prohibited from participating or
influencing the decision in city council meetings no matter how
the issue is raised. Moreover, you may not contact any members or
employees of the city to influence the decision.

However, as noted above you may (1) talk to the press and
public outside of the meeting, and you may (2) address the city
council with respect to your personal interests in the meeting.
However, where the latter exception does apply, your comments must
be limited to your personal interests, and care should be taken to
clarify that you are not acting in any official capacity. Thus,
the exception would not permit you to appear before the city

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 3


council to represent any other person's interests or the interests
of any group.

     3. The Act would not prohibit you from obtaining information
otherwise available to the public. However, other contacts
regarding a decision in which you have a conflict of interest may
be considered "influencing" the decision.

     4. Your spouse may participate freely in the referendum so
long as he is not acting as your agent.

     5. The Act does not prevent your communication with the
public or press (including letters to the editor) regarding a
matter for which you have a conflict of interest. Consequently,
even though you may not vote on issues relating to the Headlands
Plan, nor may you influence city councilmembers or staff,
you may campaign on behalf of the referendum. Thus, you are also
permitted to speak at public meetings on the subject and forums so
long as councilmembers and city staff are not present. In
addition, you may sign ballot arguments in favor of the
referendum, and send out mailings concerning its passage.

     However, the committee may be considered your controlled
committee under some circumstances, as discussed below.

B.  Solag Disposal Waste Co.

     1. Both income and loans must be disclosed on your assuming
office statement. However, with respect to income to a business
entity, you need only disclose the source if the amount paid and
loaned over the past 12 months is $10,000 or more. Since this is
not the case, Solag would not be disclosed.

     2. If the amount owed to you by Solag and the amount paid to
you over the past 12 months exceeds $250, you are prohibited from
participating in decisions that affect Solag. Thus, you have a
conflict of interest in the contract decision.

                              FACTS²

     You own a condominium in a four-unit complex on Dana Strand
Road. Your real property is located within 300 feet of the
Headlands Specific Plan Area, a 121 acre area of currently
undeveloped land. In addition, you and your spouse jointly own
and operate an alarm business from the condominium.

     In June 1994, you were elected to the Dana Point City
Council. The city will be considering a variety of issues related

_______________________
² Facts from your prior request for advice, Gallagher Advice
Letter No. I-93-205, have been incorporated into this letter.

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 4


to the future use of the Headlands area. These decisions include
zoning and land use within the area, and consideration of permits
and environmental impact reports.

The previous city council adopted the Headlands Specific
Plan. However, that specific plan decision is the subject of a
referendum measure on the November 1994 ballot and could be
reversed by the voters.

In addition, you have asked about your responsibilities with
respect to decisions that may affect Solag Disposal Waste Co.
("Solag"). You stated that Solag contracts with the City of Dana
Point to provide trash collection services. Issues concerning the
costs for services under the contract will be brought to the city
council in the future when the city renegotiates the contract.

Solag is a client of your alarm business. You stated that
your business installed an alarm system in Solag's administrative
offices and currently contracts with Solag to provide monitoring
services. You stated that Solag is being charged $23 per month
for monitoring, but has not paid any of the monitoring fees since
March 5, 1993. You stated your business has not received any
payments from Solag in the past 12 months.

ANALYSIS

Conflicts of Interest

I. Economic Interests

As we discussed in our prior letter, section 87100 of the Act
prohibits any public official from making, participating in mak-
ing, or otherwise using the official's position to influence any
decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect
on the public generally, on the official or a member of the
official's immediate family, or on any economic interest of the
official.

You have a variety of interests that may be affected by city
council decisions.

o First, your condominium is within 300 feet of the
Headlands Specific Plan Area in question. (Section 87103(b).)

o Additionally, the business you own jointly with your
spouse is operated from the condominium. (Section 87103(a) and
(d).)

o Finally, you also stated that Solag Disposal Company is a
client of your business. As noted above, you will have an
economic interest in Solag if it has been a source of income or

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 5


promised income in excess of $250 in the past 12 months. Section
82030 also includes gifts and loans in the definition of "income."
Thus, an unpaid debt obligation constitutes "income" under the Act.3
(Burnham Advice Letter No. A-87-037; Esselstein Advice Letter, No.
A-93-468.) Consequently, if the amount owed to you by Solag, and
the amount paid to you by Solag over the past 12 months in the
aggregate exceeds $250, Solag will be considered a source of
income to you.

Thus, you are prohibited from participating in any decision
that will have a reasonably foreseeable4 and material financial
effect on your condominium, your business, or Solag.

II. Materiality

As we advised previously, the Commission has adopted
differing guidelines to determine whether an effect is material,
depending on the specific circumstances of each decision.

With respect to your condominium which is within 300 feet
of the Headlands Specific Plan Area, we advised that Regulation
18702.3 states that the effect of a decision on the condominium is
material if there will be any financial effect on the value of the
condominium.

With respect to Solag, which will be directly involved in
the contract decision, Regulation 18702.1 (copy enclosed) provides
that a decision is material.5 (Regulation 18702.1(a).)


3 Both income and loans must be disclosed on your assuming office
statement. However, with respect to income to a business entity,
you need only disclose the source if the amount paid and loaned
over the past 12 months is $10,000 or more. Since this is not the
case, Solag would not be disclosed.

4 Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case. An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur. Certainty is not required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (In
re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

5 A person or business entity is directly involved in a decision
before an official's agency when that person or entity initiates
the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request; or is a named
party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation
18702.1(b).)

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 6


     o  With respect to your business which is indirectly involved
in the decision, Regulation 18702.2(g) (Copy enclosed) provides
that the effect of a decision is material if: (1) the decision
will result in an increase or decrease in the gross revenues for a
fiscal year of $10,000 or more; or (2) the decision will result in
the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $2,500 or more; or (3) the decision will result in an
increase or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities of
$10,000 or more.6

     III.  Participation

     If you have a conflict of interest, you are prohibited from
"making, participating in making, or influencing the decision.
This means that you may not vote on a matter (Regulation 18700(b),
copy enclosed) or "participate" in the decision.  (Regulation
18700(c).)  Moreover, you may not use your official position to
influence the decision by contacting or appearing before, or
otherwise attempting to influence, any member, officer, employee
or consultant of the city council, or any other city agency
subject to the city council's budgetary control.7  (Regulation
18700.1(a), copy enclosed.)

     Thus, when any decision regarding the specific plan is
considered by the city council or any other city agency, you must
abstain from any participation in the item.

     However, the Act would not prohibit you from obtaining
information otherwise available to the public.  You would,
however, be prohibited from obtaining closed session materials
regarding the matter, should there be such materials.8  Other
contacts with respect to a decision in which you have a conflict
of interest should be avoided since it may be construed to be for
the purpose of influencing the decision.

     For example, in the Torrance Advice Letter, No. A-94-084, we
stated that the prohibition on participating and influencing a
decision included a prohibition on raising issues with city staff.

     6  This assumes that your business fits under this standard.  You
should consult the regulation to determine whether any of the
prior provisions (a) – (f) apply.

     7  Please note, that when you were a member of the Capistrano Bay
Park and Recreation District you were able to appear before the
city council so long as you did not act in your official capacity.
Since you are now a member of the city council, absent an
exception, you may not appear before the city council.

     8  See, Hamilton v. City of Los Gatos (1989) 213 Cal.App.3d 1050.

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 7


[T]he exception would not allow the
councilmember to contact the staff to influence the
staff's decisions concerning the drawings by
raising issues concerning the interpretation of the
building codes. By raising such issues, the
councilmember would, in essence, be appealing the
Building and Safety Department staff's decisions,
thereby attempting to influence a decision of her
own agency. Such actions would not concern the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or
submissions.

Generally, your spouse's activity is not limited by the
provisions of the act so long as acting independently and not as
your agent. (Slaby Advice Letter, No. I-92-085.)

IV. Exceptions

A. Personal Interests

Regulation 18700.1(b) expressly provides an exception where
an otherwise disqualified official appears in the same manner as
any other member of the general public before the official's own
agency in the course of its prescribed governmental function to
represent the official's own personal interests.9

Where the exception does apply, your comments must be limited
to your personal interests, and care should be taken to clarify
that you are not acting in any official capacity. (Larsen Advice
Letter, No. A-87-151.) Thus, the exception would not permit you
to appear before the city council to represent any other person's
interests or the interests of any group.

B. Communication with the Public or the Press

In addition, Regulation·18700.1(b)(2) provides that where you
communicate with the general public or the press, such
communications are not considered an attempt to use your position
to influence a governmental decision, whether you speak regarding
your own interest or the interests of others. This would include
speaking at other public meetings of the city.

9 Regulation 18700.1(b)(1) defines an official's "personal
interests" as (1) an interest in real property which is wholly
owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family;
(2) a business entity wholly owned by the official or members of
his or her immediate family; or (3) a business entity over which
the official exercises sole direction and control, or over which
the official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction
and control.

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 8




The Referendum

     As stated above, the Act does not prevent your communication
with the public or press (including letters to the editor)
regarding a matter in which you have a conflict of interest.
Consequently, while you may not vote on issues relating to the
Headlands Specific Plan, nor could you influence city
councilmembers or staff, you may campaign in your private capacity
with personal funds¹⁰ for the referendum.  Thus, you are also
permitted to speak at public meetings on the subject and forums so
long as councilmembers and city staff are not present.  In
addition, you may sign ballot arguments in favor of the referendum
and send out mailings concerning its passage.¹¹

     Please note, however, that your involvement with a committee
organized to support or oppose the measure may result in that
committee being your candidate controlled committee.¹²  Section
82016 defines "controlled committee" as:

     [A] committee which is controlled directly or
     indirectly by a candidate or state measure
     proponent or which acts jointly with a candidate,
     controlled committee or state measure proponent in
     connection with the making of expenditures.  A
     candidate or state measure proponent controls a
     committee if he, his agent or any other committee
     he controls has a significant influence on the
     actions or decisions of the committee.

     Thus, if you exert significant influence on the actions or
decisions of the committee, or act jointly with the committee in
making expenditures, it will be treated as your controlled
committee.  For example, in the Madden Advice Letter, No. A-85-
197, we discussed several factual scenarios with respect to this
definition.  That letter concluded that attending or assisting in


_________________________
     ¹⁰ The California Penal Code prohibits use of public funds for
campaign purposes.  (See Penal Code Section 424; Stanson v. Mott
(1976) 17 Cal.3d 206; Regulation 18420.)

     ¹¹ Campaign funds may be used for this purpose and the
expenditures must be disclosed on your campaign reports.

     ¹² A candidate may have only one campaign bank account and one
controlled committee for each campaign.  (Riddle Advice Letter,
No. A-88-409.)

Our File No. I-94-279
Page 9


the fundraising activities of an independent committee, in itself,
would not indicate control of the committee by the candidate.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.13

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division

ENCLOSURES




13
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

No. A-87-151.)
Thus, the exception would not permit you
to appear before the city council to represent any other person’s
interests or the interests of any group.
B.
Communication with the Public or the Press
In addition, Regu1ation187O0.1(b) (2)
provides that where you
communicate with the general-public or-the.press,
such
communications are not considered an attempt to use your position
to influence a governmental decision, whether you speak regarding
your own interest or the interests of others.
This would include
speaking at other public meetings of the city.
Regulation 18700.1(b) (1)
defines an official’s “personal
interests” as
(1)
an interest in real property which is wholly
owned by the official or members of his or her immediate family;
(2)
a business entity wholly owned by the official or members of
his or her immediate family; or
(3)
a business entity over which
the official exercises sole direction and’control, or over which
the official and his or her spouse jointly exercise sole direction
and control.


Our File No.
1—94—279
The Referendum
As stated above, the Act does not prevent your communication
with the public or press (including letters to the editor)
regarding a matter in which you have a conflict of interest.
Consequently, while you may not vote on issues relating to the
Headlands Specific Plan, nor could you influence city
councilmembers or staff, you may campaign in your private capacity
with personal funds
1
° for the referendum.
Thus, you are also
permitted to speak at public meetings on the subject and forums so
long as councilmembers and city staff are not present.
In
addition, you may sign ballot arguments in favor of the referendum
and send out mailings concerning its passage.
11
Please note,
however, that your involvement with a committee
organized to support or oppose the measure may result in that
committee being your candidate controlled committee.
12
Section
82016 defines “controlled committee” as:
[A)
committee which is controlled directly or
indirectly by a candidate or state measure
proponent or which acts jointly with a candidate,
controlled committee or state measure proponent in
connection with the making of expenditures.
A
candidate or state measure proponent controls a
committee if he, his agent or any other committee
he controls has a significant influence on the
actions or decisions of the committee.
Thus, if you exert significant influence on the actions or
decisions of the committee, or act jointly with the committee in
making expenditures, it will be treated as your controlled
committee.
For example,
in the Madden Advice Letter,
No. A-85-
197,
we discussed several factual scenarios with respect to this
definition.
That letter concluded that attending or assisting in
10
The California Penal Code prohibits use of public funds for
campaign purposes.
(See Penal Code Section 424; Stanson v. Mott
(1976)
17 Cal.3d 206; Regulation 18420.)
Campaign funds may be used for this purpose and the
expenditures must be disclosed on your campaign reports.
12
A candidate may have only one campaign bank account and one
controlled committee for each campaign.
(Riddle Advice Letter,
No.
A—88—409.)


Our File No.
1—94—279
the fundraising activities of an independent committee,
in itself,
would not indicate control of the committee by the candidate.

CONCLUSION: A.
Headlands Specific Plan
1 and 2.
You are prohibited from participating or
influencing the decision in city council meetings no matter how
the issue is raised.
Moreover,
you may not contact any members or
employees of the city to influence the decision.
However,
as noted above you may

public outside of the meeting,
and you may
(2)
address the city
council with respect to your personal interests in the meeting.

clarify that you are not acting in any official capacity.

council to represent any other person’s interests or the interests

regarding a decision in which you have a conflict of interest may

long as he is not acting as your agent.

you have a

Gallagher Councilmember City of Dana Point 34365 Dana Strand Road #3 Dana Point, CA 92629 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-94-279 Dear Councilmember Gallagher: This is in response to your letter requesting advice regard- ing your responsibilities as a member of the Dana Point City Council under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTIONS A.
